commentary.hc2:
Headline CPI ran at 4.27% YoY in May 2026, well above the 2.96% YoY core print, with the gap driven almost entirely by an energy complex that surged 22.10% YoY and added 3.88% in a single month.

Gasoline (+40.46% YoY, +7.04% MoM) and electricity (+36.29% YoY) were the dominant upside contributors, while core goods remained largely subdued—new vehicles were essentially flat (+0.24% YoY) and used cars and trucks fell 1.99% YoY. Core services pressure was moderate, with shelter at +3.62% YoY and motor vehicle maintenance and repair at +5.98% YoY, though motor vehicle insurance turned slightly deflationary on a 12-month basis (-0.80% YoY).

Food added a contained 3.30% YoY, with food at home nearly a full percentage point softer than food away from home on the headline read.

---

• Tire industry: The 40.46% YoY surge in gasoline prices raises
commentary.mfg2:
Electricity costs rose sharply at 36.29% year-over-year, far outpacing the other components, while natural gas increased at a more moderate 5.75%. Food at home and household furnishings posted comparatively contained gains of 3.02% and 3.33% respectively, clustering near each other at the lower end of the range. The spread between the highest and lowest components exceeds 33 percentage points, indicating uneven inflation pressure across energy and goods categories.

• Tire industry: The 36.29% surge in electricity costs directly pressures manufacturing plant operating expenses, given that tire production is an energy-intensive process reliant on high-heat curing and mixing operations.
commentary.transp2:
Gasoline prices rose 40.46% year-over-year in May 2026, representing the sharpest increase among the reported transportation cost components. Airline fares followed with a 23.95% gain, while fuel oil posted a comparatively modest 4.62% increase. Motor vehicle insurance was the sole component to decline, falling 0.80% year-over-year.

• Tire industry: The 40.46% surge in gasoline costs raises fleet operators' total cost of ownership materially, which historically compresses discretionary maintenance and replacement spending and may delay tire purchasing cycles across commercial and consumer segments.
commentary.fed2:
Headline CPI running 227 basis points above the 2% target, with core at nearly 3%, indicates that underlying price pressures have not fully returned to goal. The month-over-month reading of 0.47% annualizes to roughly 5.6%, signaling that near-term momentum remains elevated rather than decelerating toward target. At these levels, the data provides limited basis for easing and is more consistent with a "hold" or restrictive bias in the federal funds rate.

• Tire industry: Persistent above-target inflation and the associated higher-for-longer rate environment keeps borrowing costs elevated for both fleet operators and consumers financing vehicle purchases, sustaining pressure on tire demand volumes and the cost of carrying raw-material and finished-goods inventory.
commentary.lens2:
Headline CPI ran at 4.27% year-over-year in May 2026, well above the core reading of 2.96%, with the gap driven almost entirely by energy. Gasoline (+40.46% YoY, +7.04% MoM) and electricity (+36.29% YoY, +3.35% MoM) are the dominant sources of inflation in the index. Airline fares (+23.95% YoY) add a secondary transport-cost pressure. Food inflation is present but moderate, with grocery prices (food at home +3.02% YoY) rising more slowly than the headline. Shelter remains elevated at +3.62% YoY but is decelerating on a monthly basis (+0.32% MoM). Vehicle-related consumer goods are disinflationary or deflationary: new vehicles are essentially flat (+0.24% YoY, -0.26% MoM), used cars and trucks are in outright deflation (-1.99% YoY), and motor vehicle insurance has turned negative (-0.80% YoY). Motor vehicle maintenance and repair (+5.98% YoY) is the one vehicle-adjacent category still posting above-core gains, consistent with elevated labor and parts costs in the service sector.

---

• Energy costs—gasoline up 40% and electricity up 36% year-over-year—are absorbing a disproportionate share of household budgets, compressing discretionary spending capacity.
• Vehicle acquisition costs (new and used) are flat to negative year-over-year, signaling weak pricing power at
commentary.tire2:
**Auto & Tire CPI Channel — May 2026**

• New vehicle prices rose 0.24% year-over-year, indicating near-flat nominal pricing with demand and inventory conditions broadly in equilibrium; used vehicle prices fell 1.99% year-over-year, continuing the post-pandemic normalization of the secondary market and compressing the price premium that had historically supported new-vehicle substitution.

• Motor vehicle maintenance & repair inflation ran at 5.98% year-over-year, the sharpest mover in the channel, reflecting persistent labor cost pressure in service bays and elevated parts pricing; motor vehicle insurance declined 0.80% year-over-year, the first meaningful deceleration in a category that had been a sustained inflation driver, suggesting underwriters are beginning to recalibrate loss-cost assumptions.

• The divergence between a near-zero new-vehicle price environment and a 5.98% maintenance cost environment indicates that the cost of ownership is shifting away from acquisition and toward upkeep, a dynamic that historically correlates with consumers extending vehicle hold periods rather than cycling into new purchases.

---

• **Tire industry:** The 5.98% maintenance & repair inflation reading, combined with consumers holding vehicles longer as acquisition costs stabilize and used-vehicle values soften, points to sustained demand for replacement tires as fleets age—providing a volume-supportive backdrop for the replacement tire segment even in a subdued new-vehicle sales environment.
commentary.ppi2:
Final demand producer prices rose 6.42% year-over-year in May 2026, with a 1.06% monthly gain.

Goods inflation significantly outpaced services: final demand goods climbed 10.25% YoY (+2.78% MoM), driven heavily by energy (+35.84% YoY, +10.70% MoM), while goods less foods and energy still ran at 5.12% YoY; final demand services rose a comparatively moderate 4.89% YoY (+0.33% MoM), with transportation and warehousing the standout component at +14.22% YoY. The pipeline carries additional pressure: processed goods for intermediate demand were up 13.13% YoY (+3.53% MoM) and unprocessed goods for intermediate demand surged 22.41% YoY (+4.92% MoM), signaling that upstream cost inflation remains well ahead of what has so far passed through to final demand.

On tire-relevant inputs (all NSA), synthetic rubber accelerated sharply at +17.78% YoY (+4.73% MoM), rubber and plastic products rose 4.18% YoY (+1.64% MoM), while finished tires and tubes increased a more modest 2.95% YoY
commentary.ppilens2:
Synthetic rubber producer prices rose 17.78% year-over-year in May 2026, running sharply ahead of downstream finished-goods indexes. Passenger car tire prices advanced 3.57% YoY and the broader tires & tubes category gained 2.95% YoY, both well below the synthetic rubber input rate, indicating that the upstream cost pressure has not passed through fully to finished tire prices. Rubber & plastic products as a whole posted 4.18% YoY inflation, sitting between the synthetic rubber input level and the finished tire categories.

• Synthetic rubber (NSA, May 2026): +17.78% YoY, the steepest move in the cost stack and the dominant upstream pressure point for tire compounding.
• Finished tire categories (NSA, May 2026): passenger car tires +3.57% YoY; tires & tubes +3.57% +2.95% YoY — both running at roughly one-fifth the pace of synthetic rubber input inflation, reflecting a widening gap between input costs and realized selling prices.
• Rubber & plastic products aggregate (NSA, May 2026): +4.18% YoY, modestly above finished tire indexes and consistent with broader polymer-chain cost elevation beyond tires alone.

• Tire industry: with synthetic rubber costs rising nearly six times faster than finished tire selling prices on a YoY basis, margin compression at the compounding and manufacturing stage is the primary financial stress point for a tire and transportation-equipment producer operating under current input conditions.
